main_paper.raw: {"methods":["FS-WLasso: a forced-sampling-based algorithm for sparse linear contextual bandits using the Lasso estimator with a compatibility condition on the optimal arm","Novel high-probability analysis technique using mathematical induction for regret analysis"],"problems":["Sparse linear contextual bandit problem with high-dimensional context features, where only a sparse subset affects the expected reward","Achieving polylogarithmic regret in both dimension (d) and time horizon (T) under weaker assumptions than existing Lasso bandit literature"],"datasets":["Synthetic datasets (numerical experiments are mentioned, but no real-world datasets are specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Regret (specifically, cumulative regret bounds)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret bound","value":"O(s^2 (log(dT)) log T)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes the weakest (mildest) known assumption for achieving O(poly log dT) regret in sparse linear contextual bandits under the single-parameter setting: compatibility condition on the optimal arm combined with the margin condition, without requiring additional diversity assumptions on context features.","Introduces FS-WLasso, a forced-sampling-based algorithm that achieves O(poly log dT) regret under these weaker assumptions.","Develops a novel high-probability analysis technique using mathematical induction to analyze regret, which can be of independent interest for other bandit algorithms.","Demonstrates through numerical experiments that the proposed algorithm consistently outperforms existing methods, even when standard diversity assumptions do not hold."]}

methods: FS-WLasso: a forced-sampling-based algorithm for sparse linear contextual bandits using the Lasso estimator with a compatibility condition on the optimal arm
Novel high-probability analysis technique using mathematical induction for regret analysis
problems: Sparse linear contextual bandit problem with high-dimensional context features, where only a sparse subset affects the expected reward
Achieving polylogarithmic regret in both dimension (d) and time horizon (T) under weaker assumptions than existing Lasso bandit literature
datasets: Synthetic datasets (numerical experiments are mentioned, but no real-world datasets are specified in the provided content)
novelty_claims: Proposes the weakest (mildest) known assumption for achieving O(poly log dT) regret in sparse linear contextual bandits under the single-parameter setting: compatibility condition on the optimal arm combined with the margin condition, without requiring additional diversity assumptions on context features.
Introduces FS-WLasso, a forced-sampling-based algorithm that achieves O(poly log dT) regret under these weaker assumptions.
Develops a novel high-probability analysis technique using mathematical induction to analyze regret, which can be of independent interest for other bandit algorithms.
Demonstrates through numerical experiments that the proposed algorithm consistently outperforms existing methods, even when standard diversity assumptions do not hold.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Doubly-Robust Lasso Bandit algorithm","Doubly-robust estimation technique from missing data literature","Lasso regression for sparse high-dimensional parameter estimation"],"problems":["Contextual multi-armed bandit with high-dimensional, sparse context features","Regret minimization in stochastic linear contextual bandit problems where only a sparse subset of features is relevant","Handling correlated contexts across arms and large/changing arm sets"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Regret (cumulative gap between optimal and chosen rewards)","High-probability regret upper bound"],"results":[{"metric":"High-probability regret upper bound","value":"O(s_0 log(dT) sqrt(T)), does not depend on number of arms N and scales with log(d)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new linear contextual MAB algorithm for high-dimensional, sparse reward models that is simple and requires fewer tuning parameters than previous works.","Introduces a new estimator for the regression parameter using Lasso with context information of all arms via the doubly-robust technique.","Proves a regret bound of O(s_0 log(dT) sqrt(T)) that does not depend on the number of arms and scales with log(d), improving over previous methods that scale polynomially with d or linearly with N.","Demonstrates that the proposed method performs well when the number of arms is large and contexts are correlated, and requires less hyperparameter tuning than Lasso Bandit and MCP Bandit."]}

methods: Doubly-Robust Lasso Bandit algorithm
Doubly-robust estimation technique from missing data literature
Lasso regression for sparse high-dimensional parameter estimation
problems: Contextual multi-armed bandit with high-dimensional, sparse context features
Regret minimization in stochastic linear contextual bandit problems where only a sparse subset of features is relevant
Handling correlated contexts across arms and large/changing arm sets
metrics: Regret (cumulative gap between optimal and chosen rewards)

results.value: O(s_0 log(dT) sqrt(T)), does not depend on number of arms N and scales with log(d)

novelty_claims: Proposes a new linear contextual MAB algorithm for high-dimensional, sparse reward models that is simple and requires fewer tuning parameters than previous works.
Introduces a new estimator for the regression parameter using Lasso with context information of all arms via the doubly-robust technique.
Proves a regret bound of O(s_0 log(dT) sqrt(T)) that does not depend on the number of arms and scales with log(d), improving over previous methods that scale polynomially with d or linearly with N.
Demonstrates that the proposed method performs well when the number of arms is large and contexts are correlated, and requires less hyperparameter tuning than Lasso Bandit and MCP Bandit.

methods: Variance-Aware Sparse Linear Bandit (VASLB) framework: a general reduction from variance-aware linear bandits to variance-aware sparse linear bandits in a black-box manner
Plug-in of VOFUL (Variance-Of-Unknown-FUL) algorithm for unknown-variance cases (computationally inefficient)
Plug-in of Weighted OFUL algorithm for known-variance cases (computationally efficient)
problems: Obtaining regret bounds for sparse linear bandits that adapt to the noise variance (variance-aware regret)
Bridging the gap between worst-case and deterministic (noiseless) regret in sparse linear bandits
Designing algorithms for sparse linear bandits whose regret interpolates between worst-case and dimension-free bounds depending on noise
novelty_claims: First variance-aware regret bounds for sparse linear bandits, interpolating between worst-case and deterministic regimes
A general black-box reduction framework (VASLB) that converts any variance-aware linear bandit algorithm into a variance-aware sparse linear bandit algorithm with little overhead in regret
Demonstration that the framework preserves variance-awareness and can achieve dimension-free regret in the deterministic (noiseless) case
Plug-in of existing variance-aware linear bandit algorithms (VOFUL and Weighted OFUL) to obtain new regret guarantees for sparse linear bandits

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PopArt (Population covariance regression with hARd Thresholding): a novel sparse linear estimation method","A convex and computationally efficient experimental design criterion based on PopArt's error bound","Sparse linear bandit algorithms based on PopArt and the new design criterion","Algorithmic symmetrization technique for lower bound proofs"],"problems":["Efficient and accurate sparse linear regression in high-dimensional settings","Designing computationally efficient algorithms for sparse linear bandits with generic (arbitrary) arm sets","Closing the gap between upper and lower regret bounds in sparse linear bandits, especially in the data-poor regime","Improving experimental design for optimal sparse linear bandits"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["\u000b\u000b1-norm recovery error (for regression)","\u000bRegret (for bandit algorithms)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["PopArt estimator provides a tighter \u000b\u000b1-norm recovery guarantee than Lasso in many problems.","The experimental design criterion based on PopArt is convex and computationally efficient, and leads to improved error bounds over previous work.","The regret upper bound for sparse linear bandits using PopArt is improved compared to prior state-of-the-art, especially with respect to the geometry of the action set.","A matching lower bound for regret in the data-poor regime is proved, closing the gap between upper and lower bounds in prior work.","The lower bound proof uses a novel application of the algorithmic symmetrization technique."]}

methods: PopArt (Population covariance regression with hARd Thresholding): a novel sparse linear estimation method
A convex and computationally efficient experimental design criterion based on PopArt's error bound
Sparse linear bandit algorithms based on PopArt and the new design criterion
Algorithmic symmetrization technique for lower bound proofs
problems: Efficient and accurate sparse linear regression in high-dimensional settings
Designing computationally efficient algorithms for sparse linear bandits with generic (arbitrary) arm sets
Closing the gap between upper and lower regret bounds in sparse linear bandits, especially in the data-poor regime
Improving experimental design for optimal sparse linear bandits
novelty_claims: PopArt estimator provides a tighter 1-norm recovery guarantee than Lasso in many problems.
The experimental design criterion based on PopArt is convex and computationally efficient, and leads to improved error bounds over previous work.
The regret upper bound for sparse linear bandits using PopArt is improved compared to prior state-of-the-art, especially with respect to the geometry of the action set.
A matching lower bound for regret in the data-poor regime is proved, closing the gap between upper and lower bounds in prior work.
The lower bound proof uses a novel application of the algorithmic symmetrization technique.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Explore-the-sparsity-then-commit algorithm: first solves a convex optimization problem to find the most informative exploration design, explores by sampling from this design, uses Lasso for parameter estimation, then commits to greedy action selection.","Post-model selection linear bandits algorithm: uses a regularized estimator for model selection, then applies a phase-elimination algorithm to the selected model."],"problems":["Regret minimization in high-dimensional stochastic linear bandits with sparse features, especially in the data-poor regime (horizon smaller than ambient dimension).","Establishing minimax regret bounds for sparse linear bandits in both data-poor and data-rich regimes."],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Regret (minimax regret, upper and lower bounds)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret lower bound (data-poor regime)","value":"Ω(C_min^{-1/3} s^{1/3} n^{2/3})"},{"metric":"Regret upper bound (explore-then-commit, data-poor regime)","value":"O(C_min^{-2/3} s^{2/3} n^{2/3})"},{"metric":"Regret upper bound (post-model selection, sufficient signal, data-rich regime)","value":"O(C_min^{-1/2} √(s n))"}],"novelty_claims":["Derivation of a novel Ω(n^{2/3}) dimension-free minimax regret lower bound for sparse linear bandits in the data-poor regime, depending on the minimum eigenvalue of the exploration distribution.","A nearly matching O(n^{2/3}) regret upper bound for an explore-then-commit algorithm in the data-poor regime, showing optimality of the rate.","Introduction of a dimension-free O(√n) regret upper bound under an additional minimum signal assumption, even in high dimensions.","Refinement of minimax regret bounds to depend on the minimum eigenvalue of the exploration distribution, rather than the ambient dimension."]}

methods: Explore-the-sparsity-then-commit algorithm: first solves a convex optimization problem to find the most informative exploration design, explores by sampling from this design, uses Lasso for parameter estimation, then commits to greedy action selection.
Post-model selection linear bandits algorithm: uses a regularized estimator for model selection, then applies a phase-elimination algorithm to the selected model.
problems: Regret minimization in high-dimensional stochastic linear bandits with sparse features, especially in the data-poor regime (horizon smaller than ambient dimension).

metrics: Regret (minimax regret, upper and lower bounds)

results.metric: Regret upper bound (post-model selection, sufficient signal, data-rich regime)

novelty_claims: Derivation of a novel Ω(n^{2/3}) dimension-free minimax regret lower bound for sparse linear bandits in the data-poor regime, depending on the minimum eigenvalue of the exploration distribution.
A nearly matching O(n^{2/3}) regret upper bound for an explore-then-commit algorithm in the data-poor regime, showing optimality of the rate.
Introduction of a dimension-free O(√n) regret upper bound under an additional minimum signal assumption, even in high dimensions.
Refinement of minimax regret bounds to depend on the minimum eigenvalue of the exploration distribution, rather than the ambient dimension.

results.value: CTL outperforms Thresholded Lasso, Sparsity-Agnostic Lasso, Doubly-Robust Lasso, and multi-agent low-dimensional algorithm [10]

novelty_claims: First algorithm to tackle row-wise distributed data in sparse linear bandits with high-dimensional features.
Proposes a cooperative multi-agent sparse linear bandit algorithm that does not require prior knowledge of the sparse structure.
Integrates information sharing among agents into high-dimensional linear bandit algorithms with both centralized and decentralized communication frameworks.
Achieves a regret bound of O(s_0 log d + s_0 sqrt(T)) and communication cost of O(s_0 log T), balancing communication and computation costs.
Demonstrates superior empirical performance compared to state-of-the-art single and multi-agent methods under minimal assumptions.

methods: VACDB algorithm (Variance-Aware Contextual Dueling Bandit)
SupLinUCB-type algorithm adapted for dueling bandits with variance-aware regret analysis

Symmetric arm selection strategy
problems: Lack of variance-aware regret bounds in stochastic contextual dueling bandits
Designing efficient algorithms for contextual dueling bandits that adapt to the variance in pairwise comparisons

novelty_claims: First variance-aware regret bound for stochastic contextual dueling bandits under a generalized linear model.
Proposed VACDB algorithm achieves regret bounds that adapt to the variance of pairwise comparisons, improving over previous variance-agnostic approaches.
Algorithm is computationally efficient and does not require prior knowledge of the variance.
Empirical results demonstrate the advantage of the proposed method over strong baselines in environments with varying variances.
Provided a rigorous proof for a technical gap in the analysis of MLE for generalized linear bandits using Brouwer's invariance of domain property.

Regret bounds in the batched setting are comparable to those in the fully sequential setting, despite using only \mathcal{O}(\log T) batches.

methods: A computationally efficient algorithm for linear bandits with heteroscedastic noise and unknown variance, based on adaptive variance-aware confidence sets and a multi-layer structure for context stratification
A new Freedman-type concentration inequality for vector-valued self-normalized martingales with heteroscedastic random variables
An adaptive variance-aware exploration strategy using self-adaptive confidence sets updated by online estimator's square loss
Extension of the above techniques to reinforcement learning via a variance-adaptive algorithm for linear mixture Markov decision processes (MDPs), named UCRL-AVE
problems: Designing computationally efficient algorithms for linear bandits with heteroscedastic (round-dependent) noise and unknown variance, achieving variance-dependent regret bounds
Obtaining variance-dependent, problem-adaptive regret bounds for linear mixture MDPs in reinforcement learning, without requiring prior knowledge of noise variance or explicit variance estimation
novelty_claims: First computationally efficient algorithm for linear bandits with heteroscedastic noise and unknown variance, achieving variance-dependent regret bounds without prior knowledge of the noise variance
Development of a new Freedman-type concentration inequality for vector-valued self-normalized martingales with heteroscedastic random variables, generalizing previous results
Introduction of a multi-layer structure for context stratification and adaptive variance-aware confidence sets for exploration in linear bandits

Extension of these techniques to reinforcement learning, providing a variance-adaptive algorithm (UCRL-AVE) for linear mixture MDPs that achieves problem-dependent, horizon-free regret bounds without explicit variance estimation or high-order moment estimators
The regret bounds are minimax optimal in both deterministic and worst-case regimes, and gracefully interpolate between these extremes

methods: Greedy algorithm for contextual bandits (exploration-free)
Greedy-First algorithm (starts greedy, switches to exploration if needed via hypothesis test)
problems: Reducing or eliminating the need for explicit exploration in contextual bandit algorithms, especially when exploration is costly or unethical
Determining under what conditions exploration-free (greedy) algorithms can achieve optimal regret in contextual bandits
Designing algorithms that adaptively minimize exploration while maintaining optimal learning performance
datasets: Real datasets (not specified by name in the provided content, but referenced as used in simulations and in related empirical studies)

results.value: Greedy-First outperforms standard exploration-based contextual bandit algorithms (e.g., Thompson sampling, UCB) in simulations, especially when covariate diversity does not hold
Greedy algorithm outperforms standard bandit algorithms when covariate diversity holds
Greedy-First provably reduces the expected amount of forced exploration compared to standard bandit algorithms
Greedy-First achieves rate optimal regret bounds without additional assumptions on context distribution or number of arms

novelty_claims: Proves that a simple greedy (exploration-free) algorithm can be rate optimal for contextual bandits if the context distribution satisfies covariate diversity, a new condition introduced in the paper
Shows that even without covariate diversity, a greedy algorithm can be rate optimal with positive probability, challenging the necessity of explicit exploration in many cases
Introduces the Greedy-First algorithm, which adaptively decides whether to explore based on a hypothesis test using observed data, and proves it is rate optimal without additional assumptions
Demonstrates both theoretically and empirically that Greedy-First reduces exploration and outperforms standard exploration-based contextual bandit algorithms
Provides a new interpretation and practical approach to exploration in contextual bandits, making bandit algorithms more applicable in settings where exploration is costly or unethical
